articles.author: Renae Dyer
articles.title: Dollar Rises to One-Year High on Increased Prospects of Higher U.S. Interest Rates
articles.extracted_text: The U.S. dollar surged to a one-year high, with the DXY index touching 101.127 -- its strongest level since May 2025. The euro fell to a three-month low of $1.1416. Markets now price a 90% chance of a 25-basis-point rate hike in September. The rally followed a hawkish shift from the Federal Reserve under new Chair Kevin Warsh, who held rates at 3.50%-3.75% but saw 9 of 19 officials projecting a rate hike by year-end. PCE inflation forecast raised sharply to 3.6% from 2.7%. Forward guidance was eliminated entirely. The move occurred on the Juneteenth holiday with thin liquidity. ING analyst Francesco Pesole warned the dollar rally may be overdone, as markets are overestimating the chance of a Fed hike and the US-Iran deal removes a positive argument for the dollar.

articles.title: Warsh kicks off Fed era with sweeping review as rates remain unchanged
articles.extracted_text: Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh held his first policy meeting, leaving interest rates unchanged at 3.5%-3.75% despite inflation stuck well above the 2% target. 9 out of 19 policymakers now expect a rate hike by end-2026. Warsh scrapped forward guidance entirely: 'I can't give you any forward guidance about what we're going to do next. The good news is we'll be meeting in six weeks.' He launched a five-part comprehensive review covering balance sheet, communications, data sources, productivity, and the inflation framework. PCE inflation forecast for end-2026 raised to 3.6% from 2.7%. The yield curve began flattening, with Renaissance Macro's Neil Dutta warning: 'Flattening is often the road toward inversion, which is the market's way of signaling that the policy stance is becoming restrictive enough to threaten the expansion.' BlackRock's Rick Rieder: 'This time is different. Investors will have to learn to make do with less Fed signaling.'

articles.title: Markets News, June 18, 2026: Stocks Rise to Finish Winning Week; Intel Leads Chip, Memory Stocks Higher; SpaceX Continues Slide
articles.extracted_text: Stocks rallied to close a winning week, recovering from a Fed-driven sell-off. S&P 500 rose 1.1% (+80.48 points) to 7,500.58. Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.1% to 51,564.70. Nasdaq Composite rose 1.9%. Russell 2000 rose 2.12% to 2,979.77. For the week: S&P 500 +0.9%, Nasdaq +2.4%, Dow +0.7%. Intel (INTC) jumped 11% after Trump's Truth Social post said Apple will work with Intel to design and build chips in America. Neither Apple nor Intel confirmed the deal. Intel up >450% over 12 months, recently topping its 2000 record high. Micron +9%, Marvell +8% in sector contagion. SpaceX (SPCX) fell 3.7% to ~$175 in continued post-IPO slide. IPO price was $135; hit intraday high above $225 on Tuesday. PitchBook: 'A 4.2% initial float and roughly 30% retail allocation set up 20% to 30% swings on catalysts.' Kroger (KR) -8%+ on same-store sales +1.0% (vs +3.2% prior year), EPS $1.46, gross margin compressed to 22.7%. Fed Chair Warsh hawkish debut: 2-year Treasury yield rose to 4.20%. WTI crude $76.75/bbl, Brent $79.50/bbl. 10-year yield 4.46%. Gold $4,235/oz. VIX 16.88.

articles.title: Stocks rally falters, oil rises as US-Iran talks postponed
articles.extracted_text: Switzerland announced that planned follow-up talks on the US-Iran agreement have been postponed. Israeli airstrikes in southern Lebanon killed at least 24 people, threatening the broader deal that Tehran views Lebanon as critical to. Oil immediately rebounded: WTI +1.8% at $78.01/bbl, Brent +0.9% at $80.53/bbl. Asian markets mixed: Nikkei 225 +0.3% at 71,250.06, Kospi -0.1% at 9,052.42 (reversed from record above 9,000), Hong Kong and Shanghai closed for holidays. European futures lower. USD/JPY at 161.30, near weakest since 1986, with Japan Finance Minister Katayama warning of 'bold action against excessive speculative moves.' eToro analyst Josh Gilbert: 'With the deal signed, that geopolitical cloud is lifting, but markets have learned more than once that a resolution can unravel quickly. The hard work starts now.' Forex.com analyst Fawad Razaqzada: 'With valuations still elevated and a lack of obvious near-term catalysts, the prospect of profit-taking or a modest correction has become more plausible following the Fed's hawkish pivot.' Dow previous close at 51,564.70.
articles.topic: geopolitics oil Iran Israel global session

articles.title: Oil rebounds above $80 after Israeli strikes on Lebanon, stocks turn lower
articles.extracted_text: Oil prices reversed sharp declines after Israeli airstrikes in southern Lebanon killed at least 24 people, threatening the recently signed US-Iran truce. Brent crude rose back above $80/barrel as of 5:40 a.m. GMT. WTI climbed to $76.30/barrel. Global equities and risk assets turned lower amid renewed geopolitical uncertainty. Hang Seng -1.6%, Shanghai Composite -0.4%, Euro Stoxx 50 futures -0.5%, DAX futures -0.5%, FTSE futures -0.2%. U.S. index futures traded lower. Precious metals retreated: Gold -1.6% to $4,140/oz, Silver -2.5% to ~$64/oz, Palladium -2.0% to $1,240, Platinum -1.6% to $1,660. Cryptocurrencies declined: Bitcoin -1.8% at $62,740, Ethereum -1.9% at $1,700. Total crypto market cap $2.2 trillion (-1.5%). The US-Iran deal had paved the way to reopen Strait of Hormuz, which normally handles ~20% of global oil flows. The fragile peace was already under pressure from the Fed hawkish stance before the Israeli strikes.

articles.title: Gold and Silver Keep Sliding as Rates, Not War, Drive the Metals
articles.extracted_text: Gold drifted lower again on Thursday June 18, easing toward the low $4,210s an ounce. Silver slipped into the mid-$65.70 range. Both metals fell despite the US-Iran peace deal, falling oil prices, and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. The real driver is interest rates: the Federal Reserve's signal of higher rates ahead keeps pressure on non-yielding metals. Both now sit at or below long-term trend lines. Gold at $4,183 (-0.98%), Silver at $65.21 (-1.58%). Thesis: 'A rate-driven decline wearing a safe-haven disguise. The metals are not failing as havens; they are being priced as rate-sensitive assets, and until the rate picture turns, the grind lower is likely to continue.' Cross-asset divergence: stocks rebounded post-Fed, but gold/silver could not rally because falling oil removed inflation-hedge demand and the firm rate outlook damages yield-free assets. Both metals have broken below long-term trend lines. Central bank buying remains a long-term floor.

articles.title: Intel's stock jumps 11% -- even as analysts say new Apple chip deal might start small
articles.extracted_text: Intel stock rose 10.64% to ~$133.82 on Thursday after President Trump posted on Truth Social that Apple will work with Intel to design and build chips in the U.S. Apple +0.70%, roughly flat. The Trump post follows a WSJ report from last month about a preliminary Intel-Apple chip-making agreement. The exact chips and product applications remain unclear. Apple separately announced price increases due to a memory-chip supply crunch driving up component costs. Intel shares up >550% from the U.S. government purchase price of $20.47 per share (August 2025 equity investment, 10% stake). Bernstein analyst Stacy Rasgon: 'Any early foundry relationship would likely be low-volume, less-important parts,' speculating lower-end PC chips. Tigress Financial analyst Ivan Feinseth: 'Intel is steadily expanding its domestic capacity and converting political and strategic tailwinds into concrete foundry wins with blue-chip customers.' Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan said on a T. Rowe Price podcast that the U.S. government has been 'a good cheerleader and supportive. And that's what I need.' Intel recently topped its 2000 record high near $121.

articles.title: US Stock Market Holiday Schedule -- Juneteenth Closed
articles.extracted_text: The NYSE and Nasdaq are closed on Friday, June 19, 2026, in observance of the Juneteenth National Independence Day federal holiday. Both major stock exchanges are fully closed. This is an official market holiday per NASDAQ's published holiday schedule.

articles.title: How major US stock indexes fared Thursday 6/18/2026
articles.extracted_text: Stocks closed higher on Wall Street Thursday, June 18, 2026. The S&P 500 rose 1.1% (+80.48 points) to 7,500.58. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.1% (+51.24 points) to 51,564.70. The Nasdaq Composite rose 1.9% (+501.34 points) to 26,517.93. The Russell 2000 index of smaller companies rose 2.1% (+61.87 points) to 2,979.77. U.S. equity and bond markets closed early ahead of the Juneteenth holiday on Friday, June 19.
